title: Reactive Power Compensation in Medium-Voltage High-Power Current Source Rectifier-Fed Topologies
authors: Pratyush Pandey, Harikrishna P., Kamalesh Hatua, Subhashish Bhattacharya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: High-power applications use Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)-based converters because of their robustness and low conduction losses. These applications include DC supplies for electrolytic processes, traction, and other drives or loads that demand high power control. However, these applications face challenges with reactive power demand and harmonic distortions, especially under light load conditions or, in the case of drives, at lower speeds and torque. This article proposes a solution that uses a three-phase four-winding Wye-Wye-Delta-Wye transformer with a low-power, low-voltage source inverter (LV-VSI). Using LV-VSI-assisted commutation, one SCR rectifier operates at a leading power factor and the other operates at a lagging power factor, canceling the fundamental reactive power demands and achieving a unity power factor at the grid. This strategy is valid for any load across rectifiers. The experiment is conducted with a current-source inverter-fed hybrid motor drive load. Simulation and experimental results validate the proposed strategy to reduce reactive power while maintaining the simplicity and reliability of SCR-based rectifiers.

title: Nonsmooth Loop Filter for Phase-Locked Loops: Design, Analysis, and Validation
authors: Ke Yu, Weile Chen, Fuyuan Xiang, Shihua Li, Jinya Su, Haoyong Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Classical phase-locked loops (PLLs) employing proportional–integral (PI) loop filters achieve only asymptotic convergence, resulting in limited transient speed and theoretically infinite settling time. To address these limitations, this article proposes a nonsmooth loop filter for fast and accurate PLL operation. A rigorous Lyapunov-based analysis is conducted, showing finite-time stabilization under different position reference signal inputs. The proposed nonsmooth PLL is further validated through comparative simulations and experiments conducted on a 2 kW interior permanent magnet synchronous motor (IPMSM) platform, showing faster phase and frequency convergence (up to 27.5% improvement in recovery time under speed changes) and smaller estimation error (up to 32% improvement in position error under speed changes) compared with conventional PI-based PLLs. Owing to its concise structure and analytically verified stability, the proposed nonsmooth PLL is expected to provide a practical and scalable solution for high-performance industrial electronic systems.

title: Toward FPGA Cloud Security: A Ring Oscillator-free and Low-power Attack Circuit
authors: She Tang, Zhiyuan Bai, Zhe Chen, Jian Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cloud Computing
abstract: Since FPGA clouds play an important role in cutting-edge domains as computing infrastructures, their security has attracted widespread attention from security researchers. In our paper, we study a novel attack circuit that can bypass defence techniques to conduct denial-of-service (DoS) attacks on FPGA clouds, prompting researchers to enhance the defence techniques of FPGA clouds. First, we design a tree-based attack circuit that does not include ring oscillator structures and nodes with high fan-out values. Therefore, our attack circuit can elude structure detection. Then, we propose a minimal resource search algorithm based on the power and temperature constraints to reduce FPGA power and temperature by shrinking the scale of our attack circuit. With this approach, our circuit can bypass the dynamic monitoring of FPGA clouds. In order to verify the effectiveness of our attack circuit, we carry out DoS attacks on multiple types of FPGAs, including FPGAs deployed in cloud platforms. The experimental results show that our attack circuit can successfully crash these FPGAs, and pose a serious threat to FPGA clouds. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our attack circuit has the advantage of covertness compared to other DoS attack circuits.

title: Performance Enhancement of an Interleaved Parallel Three-Coupled Inductor Boost Converter
authors: Ze Wang, Qunhai Huo, Zhaowei Liu, Yuanli Lu, Wenli Lin, Lixin Wu, Jingyuan Yin, Tongzhen Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: With the continuous development of aerospace science and technology, the construction of space power plants has become an important research direction in the current development of space technology. The study of high-power DC-DC converters in space is part of the core technologies that restrict the growth of space power plants. To meet the development needs of modern space DC-DC power systems for high-power, high efficiency, and high anti-interference, this paper proposes an interleaved parallel three-coupled inductor boost converter. Multiple coupled inductor technology is used to integrate three inductors on the same magnetic core, and based on retaining the original topology characteristics, phase-to-phase coupling is achieved, which improves the steady-state and transient performance of the converter; The use of interleaved parallel technology greatly reduces the ripple of input current, lowers the current stress of the converter switching transistor (i.e., the semiconductor power devices), reduces switching losses, improves the overall efficiency of the converter, and retains the advantages of the original topology minimum phase system. Finally, a 5kW experimental prototype was under construction and the performance of the proposed converter was experimentally verified.

title: COSMOS-RV: A Lightweight Single-Issue Speculative Out-of-Order 32-Bit RISC-V Processor with Virtually Indexed Physically Tagged Cache Architecture and FPGA Validation
authors: KP Jayalakshmi, Balachandra R Jogi, Y U Yashaswi, Vaishnavi Poojary, Vaishnavi, Supritha, S Adarsh Rag
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Traditional in-order RISC-V cores with 32-bit pipelines suffer from pipeline hazards, sequential issue, and memory latencies, which restrict instruction throughput and execution time. Although modern superscalar OoO RISC-V processors such as BOOM and SonicBOOM address these issues by implementing multi-issue capabilities and aggressive speculative execution, they are complicated in structure and are therefore not suitable for FPGA implementation at moderate scale.We propose COSMOS-RV, a speculative single-issue out-of-order 32-bit RISC-V processor that seeks to optimize execution performance without increasing unnecessary hardware overheads in order to be used with FPGA. Our architecture consists of a five-stage pipeline and an efficient out-of-order backend that utilizes register renaming, dynamic scheduling, reservation-based execution, reordering and precise in-order commit mechanisms. Speculative execution capabilities can be achieved by branch prediction and rollback to minimize the overhead of control hazard penalties. The memory hierarchy is composed of separated 32 KB instruction and data caches with virtually indexed physically tagged (VIPT) cache structure, coupled with memory and priority-based arbitration. Implementation has been performed using Verilog synthesis and targeted on FPGA platforms. Performance analysis was done using instruction streams and their characteristics that clearly differentiate between data, control, and memory stalls. Through experimental evidence, we see a 15.15% gain in instruction throughput and 13.16% decrease in execution latency when compared with an equally clocked five-stage in-order baseline processor running on the same memory. This improvement comes mostly from improvements in data stall penalties; however, control and memory stall improvement is minimal due to the limitation of being single-issue. The presented approach takes a look at OoO processing at its simplest, and demonstrates that there is a way to gain performance improvements without having to expand the number of issue widths or become superscalar. In effect, this represents a balance between performance and hardware expense, making COSMOS-RV a good choice between in-order soft cores and complicated OoO superscalar processors.

title: EFS-YOLO: A steel surface defect detection model fusing multi-scale edge features and frequency-domain information
authors: Pengcheng Meng, Wei Zhang, Hao Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: To resolve false and missed detections in metal surface defect detection, and to improve the model’s performance on multi-scale and complex samples, a detection method named EFS-YOLO is proposed. First, an edge information communication (EIC) module is designed, which extracts edge features from shallow network feature maps comprehensively via the multi-scale edge information generator (MSEI). The EIC module further fuses these edge features with other layers using the convolutional edge fusion (CEF) module, enhancing cross-scale target detection accuracy. Second, the C3k2-FTD module is proposed, incorporating a frequency-time domain extraction (FTD) submodule for joint time-frequency feature fusion. This enables comprehensive defect feature capture and boosts effective recognition of defect targets. Finally, to improve performance on complex samples without increasing memory or computational costs, the SlideLoss function is introduced and integrated with the exponential moving average (EMA) mechanism, directing the model to focus on misclassified and challenging samples. Experimental results show EFS-YOLO achieves an mAP of 82.1% on the NEU-DET dataset, outperforming the base model by 2.6%, with precision and recall improved by 1.3% and 2.5%, respectively. On the GC10-DET dataset, mAP increases by 2.3%. These results validate the method’s effectiveness, generalization, and efficacy in metal surface defect detection.

title: Supervision-Guided Contrastive Learning for Rolling Bearing Fault Diagnosis under Label Scarcity
authors: Xinwei Zhao, Xu Zhang, Lei Su, Ke Li
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: While deep learning has advanced industrial fault diagnosis, its reliance on massive labeled datasets remains a major obstacle to real-world application. In response to the scarcity of labeled fault samples and ineffective utilization of abundant unlabeled data, this paper proposes a novel semi-supervised fault diagnosis method based on supervision-guided contrastive learning (SGCL). First, a supervision-guided mechanism is introduced to leverage a small number of labeled samples to construct semantic class centers. This explicitly guides unlabeled data toward their respective categories, thereby alleviating the mismatch between different optimization objectives and enhancing intra-class compactness and inter-class separability. Second, a consistency regularization strategy is introduced to improve the model’s representation capability via pseudo-label constraints, enabling unlabeled representations to directly participate in updating the classifier parameters. By jointly optimizing these mechanisms, the proposed framework seamlessly translates raw unlabeled data into highly discriminative diagnostic features. Comprehensive experiments on a self-built dataset and the Paderborn dataset demonstrate that SGCL achieves diagnostic accuracies of 91.74% and 91.42%, respectively, under a 5% label ratio. This represents an average improvement of approximately 8%-10% over compared generative and contrastive semi-supervised methods, verifying the effectiveness of the proposed approach in addressing fault diagnosis tasks under severely limited label conditions.

title: How to Tailor Power-Electronic Inertia to the Constraints of its Resource
authors: Walter Schittek, Nils Wiese, Christian Hachmann, Xiuqiang He, Johannes Heid, Jurian Ferry, Martin Braun
publish: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
abstract: In recent years, research on power-electronic contributions of inherent inertia via grid-forming (GFM) control schemes has made significant progress. But several technical hurdles still stand against major progress towards an optimized use of resources and minimized cost on the way of providing enough power-electronic inertia for the next decade. Oriented at basic properties of virtual synchronous machines (VSMs), this article indicates generic technical solutions for respecting active-power (P) constraints of devices, tapping all working-point-dependent potentials of P variation for inertia, implementing RoCoF dead bands, circumventing the inherent slowness of a VSM to follow setpoint changes while retaining the GFM characteristic, and maximizing the combined P variation ranges of inertia and P(f) control. The overall objective is to tap the full P-variation potential of resources at/behind the DC link, even and particularly of the fluctuating ones. The described implementation example of tailored inertia is kept as generic as possible to allow for selective adoption and/or combination with other approaches. After explanation of the technical concepts in sufficient detail, simulations demonstrate the feasibility of tailoring inertia, yielding adequate behaviour in challenging circumstances.

abstract: This article analyzes the shift from voluntary ethical guidelines to binding “hard law” in artificial intelligence across six global jurisdictions and regions. It evaluates how this regulatory divergence compels multinational enterprises to replace centralized technology strategies with localized compliance models.

title: A Lightweight UWB Radar Multi-Person Action Recognition Method
authors: Pengyun Chen, Jiyue Wang, Jiaxin Zheng, Demin Kong, Huixi Wei, Shisheng Guo, Guolong Cui, Mingliang Xu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Ultra-wideband (UWB) radar has been widely adopted for human action recognition due to its high resolution, robustness to varying lighting conditions and insensitivity to occlusion. Current multi-target behavior recognition methods may fail to perform effectively when behavioral signal interference exists between targets. Additionally, existing behavior recognition models often have a large number of parameters, making them difficult to deploy on portable embedded platforms. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a lightweight multi-person action recognition framework LRC-Net. The LRC-Net utilizes a lightweight global information extraction module and an attention mechanism, and employs a dynamic adaptive threshold focal loss function to improve the classification performance on challenging samples. To validate the efficacy of our approach, we constructed a dedicated UWB radar-based action recognition dataset and conducted extensive experiments. The results indicate that the proposed method achieves superior performance in recognition accuracy, F1 score and parameter compared to existing algorithms. Furthermore, the framework demonstrates strong robustness in environments with both strong and weak target signal interference.

title: A Millimeter-Wave Vertically-Polarized End-Fire Antenna With Zero Clearance for Metal-Frame Mobile Terminals
authors: Xin Dai, Zi Hang Qiu, Wan Jun Yang, Yu Fei Pan, Xiu Yin Zhang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a vertically-polarized end-fire antenna tailored for millimeter-wave (mmWave) metal-frame mobile terminals. A rectangular slot is etched into the metal frame, with its outer section filled with a dielectric block to form a conformal slot antenna. Unlike the conventional substrate-integrated waveguide (SIW) feeding scheme commonly used for vertically-polarized end-fire antennas, an asymmetrical substrate-integrated coaxial line (ASICL) is embedded into the inner portion of the slot to serve as the feeding mechanism. By shorting the termination of the ASICL to ground, the quasi-TEM mode is converted to TE10 mode, enabling vertical polarization. Additionally, the shorting pin of the ASICL is strategically designed with a quarter-wave length, inherently generating a monopole mode. This dual-resonance characteristic effectively enhances the operational bandwidth of the antenna. A zero-clearance configuration is achieved through the highly integrated design of the slotted metal frame, substrate-integrated monopole, and enclosed ASICL feeding network. For validation, a 1×8 antenna array based on the proposed design is designed and fabricated. Measured results demonstrate a reflection coefficient below −10 dB across 26.8–31.7 GHz, covering 5G mmWave bands (n257/n258/n261). The proposed antenna presents an innovative and practical solution for modern terminal devices.

title: Robust mmWave Connectivity via Relay-Coordinated Path Control in Industrial Robots
authors: Yijing Ren, Vasilis Friderikos
publish: IEEE Networking Letters
abstract: To address the explosive demand for robust and reliable high-speed communications, millimetre-wave networks that utilize abundant bandwidth in the emerging sixth generation (6G) have been considered as key enablers in supporting diverse applications of industrial mobile robots. Millimeter-wave (mmWave) transmissions are highly susceptible to obstruction by physical obstacles, particularly in cluttered industrial/warehouse environments where mobile robots (MRs) such as humanoids autonomously navigate to carry out diverse tasks. Ensuring robust communication under non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions thus remains a significant and largely unresolved challenge. To this end, a novel relay selection based path planning scheme via a mixed integer linear programming formulation is proposed, where two-hop wireless links are established by selecting idle MRs as relay nodes and robots’ trajectory is jointly considered to ensure line-of-sight (LoS) communication. Extensive numerical investigations demonstrate that, compared with a shortest-path scheme that ignores NLoS propagation, the proposed relay-selection-based path-planning scheme improves the overall communication rate by 38%. Moreover, for MRs operating under NLoS conditions, the communication rate can be increased by up to a factor of six.

title: Collision-Aware Spatially Constrained Kinematic Optimization With Hierarchical Detection for the CMOR Manipulator
authors: Hongbin Huang, Haoyin Wang, Chenhui Wan, Zhongxu Hu, Jie Liu, Youmin Hu, Yang Cheng
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Remote maintenance manipulators for tokamak reactors must operate within severely constrained workspaces while sustaining high payloads and positioning accuracy. Existing kinematic optimization approaches typically decouple performance optimization from realistic workspace and collision constraints, which can yield designs that are infeasible or unstable in complex reactor environments. To address this limitation, this article proposes a spatially constrained kinematic optimization (SCKO) framework for the seven-DOF CFETR Multipurpose Overload Robot manipulator. The key contribution is the endogenous integration of high-fidelity collision detection into the optimization loop, such that kinematic performance is evaluated and optimized exclusively over collision-free configurations within the true Tokamak geometry. A collision-filtered Jacobian condition number is adopted as the optimization objective, and a hierarchical embedded collision-detection strategy is developed to enable dense Monte Carlo workspace evaluation with acceptable computational cost. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate that SCKO consistently achieves a performance improvement of up to 15.1% and a reduction in computation time of up to 34%. Prototype experiments under a 2.5-ton payload further verify that reduced condition numbers directly improve end-effector positioning accuracy and directional stability. These results establish SCKO as a practical and effective framework for kinematic design of heavy-load manipulators operating in geometrically complex environments.

title: Electric Motors Exposed to Ambient Stresses During Industrial Operations: Partial Discharge Diagnostics
authors: Waqar Hassan, Ghulam Amjad Hussain, Keyvan Firuzi, Azrul Mohd Ariffin, John A. Kay
publish: IEEE Industry Applications Magazine
abstract: The electric motor (EM) is one of the most significant electrical components used in any industrial environment. For a reliable operation of EM, it is essential to assess the health state of the stator insulation. Partial discharge (PD) diagnostics stand out as a reliable approach for evaluating the health of insulation material. During industrial operations, EMs are exposed to high levels of contamination under varying temperatures and relative humidity (RH), resulting in ambient stresses. The ambient stresses significantly contribute to the degradation of stator insulation, making PD diagnostics challenging. In this article, the effect of contamination under varying temperature and RH in electrical EM insulation is investigated through PD diagnostics. The variations in PD intensity is investigated under varying environmental conditions. PD measurements were carried out within an artificial industrial environment inside a climate-controlled chamber. Various PD features were extracted to study probability distribution functions to further investigate the PD severity at various levels of contamination, under varying temperatures and RH. Based on the experimental results, it was revealed that PD activity within the EM insulation was significantly affected by temperature and RH under varying contamination conditions. The proposed methodology is believed to be helpful in reliability assessments for a wide range of industrial applications.

title: A Stiffness-Adaptive Visuo-Tactile Robotic Palpation System for Surface Texture Profiling and Deep Lesion Localization_supp1-3691370.mp4
authors: Dongliang Fan
publish: N/A
abstract: Accurate intraoperative tissue characterization relies on the ability to resolve both surface microtextures and deep-seated structures for comprehensive diagnosis. However, achieving surface profiling and deep-tissue inspection remains a critical challenge due to the difficulty of combining variable stiffness and high-fidelity perception within a compact device. Here, we propose a robotic palpation system consisting of a novel visuo-tactile sensing probe (diameter: 10 mm) integrated into a robotic platform for accurate tissue characterization. This probe exhibits adaptive strain-stiffening behavior (achieving a 470-fold stiffness variation), applicable for various surgical conditions. With our 3-D reconstruction and scanning framework, the palpation system achieves high-precision 3-D reconstruction (MAE: 28 μm in the z-axis, 3.1 μm in the xy-plane), profiling surface textures (feature size: 150 μm), and delineating the boundaries of complex deep-seated inclusions (sensitivity $ > $ 0.92). Finally, an ex vivo porcine liver model was prepared to demonstrate our system’s capability to analyze and decouple microscale superficial irregularities from macroscale lobulated deep-seated lesions under dynamic disturbances. These results confirm the system’s potential as a precise, quantitative platform for robotic surgical palpation in complex pathological scenarios.

title: Compromised Control of DC Microgrids With ZIP Loads and Nonnegligible Line Loss
authors: Siyao Liao, Handong Bai, Chao Deng, Hongwei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: For DC microgrids with ZIP loads and nonnegligible line resistances, three essential control objectives are current sharing, voltage regulation, and line loss minimization. However, existing distributed secondary control approaches typically only address one or two of these objectives; or rely on optimization-based formulations with limited real-time applicability. To address this gap, this article proposes a distributed compromised control strategy that simultaneously coordinates current sharing, voltage regulation, and line loss minimization through a tunable control-law-level framework, without solving centralized optimization problems. A dynamic model of the closed-loop DC microgrid is established, and stability is analyzed using singular perturbation theory and root-locus analysis. Hardware-in-the-loop experiments on a four-bus DC microgrid testbed illustrate the effectiveness and adaptability of the proposed framework under representative practical operating scenarios.

title: Approximate Nonovershooting Zeta-Backstepping Control With its Applications to Quad-Rotors
authors: Jiaxing Yang, Xuebo Yang, Xiaolong Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article presents an approximate nonovershooting zeta-backstepping control method for a class of uncertain nonlinear systems. The proposed method ensures that the tracking error exhibits approximately nonovershooting characteristics by leveraging the zeta-backstepping approach to design the system with a critically damped ratio. A momentum gradient descent algorithm is employed for the neural network to estimate the effects of nonlinear uncertainty, thereby achieving faster and more robust convergence. By employing the second-order Lyapunov criterion within the zeta-backstepping framework, rigorous proofs are established for both the stability of the closed-loop system and the approximate nonovershooting performance. Finally, experimental results on a quad-rotor hover system demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme.

title: Wearable Visuomotor Navigation and Mobility Assistance Device for the Partially Sighted and Visually Impaired in Unfamiliar Indoor Scenes
authors: Zhengcai Cao, Hongmin Mu, Yiyang Sun, Ji Xia, Yinbin Shi, Zhe Ma, MengChu Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: To improve the mobility and independence of the partially sighted and visually impaired (PSVI), a variety of assistive navigation devices with simultaneous localization and mapping technologies have been proposed. However, upon entering an unfamiliar indoor scene, PSVI users face the challenge of lacking dense prior maps for navigation. To well address this challenge, we propose an innovative wearable navigation assistance system for them in unfamiliar indoor scenes. To construct maps required for navigation, we generate topological semantic maps (TSM) from layout maps. To utilize TSM for localization, we propose to match the detected text nodes from signs with that of TSM for positioning. To significantly improve localization accuracy and mitigate instances of node mismatch between real-world scenes and TSM, we develop glasses equipped with binocular distance measurement, thereby enabling precise navigation toward signs for PSVI users. We determine the positions of obstacles around them through visual perception. In emergency situations requiring obstacle avoidance, our designed wristbands provide immediate and intuitive vibrational feedback to guide them. We deploy our algorithms in a computer that communicates with the users through multiple wearable devices. Finally, we conduct navigation experiments in a hospital. Experimental results show that our device can assist PSVI users in completing indoor navigation tasks safely and efficiently.

title: EFSANet: Explicit Frequency Modeling and Style-Aware Alignment for Hyperspectral—Multispectral Image Fusion
authors: Dongbin Hu, Yingying Fan, Yuyin Ma, Yurong Qian, Yuanxu Wang, Sen Luo, Yi'ao Jia, Xinhui Li, Meng Zhang, S. N. Tereshchenko
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The fusion of low-resolution hyperspectral images (LR-HSIs) and high-resolution multispectral images (HR-MSIs) is an effective strategy for reconstructing high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSIs), and is important for remote sensing observation, land-cover classification, and environmental monitoring. In recent years, deep learning-based hyperspectral-multispectral fusion methods have achieved remarkable progress. However, existing methods still face two key challenges. First, differences in spectral response functions and spatial resolutions introduce significant cross-modal discrepancies, which hinder accurate feature alignment and joint modeling. Second, high-frequency structural information is often weakened during deep feature extraction and interaction, leading to blurred textures, degraded edges, and reduced spatial fidelity in reconstructed images. To address these issues, this paper proposes an Explicit Frequency Modeling and Style-Aware Alignment Network (EFSANet) for hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion. The proposed method integrates frequency-domain enhancement, spatial contextual modeling, and style-adaptive correction to reduce cross-modal discrepancies and recover fine spatial details. Specifically, the Pyramid Frequency Fusion (PFF) module explicitly decomposes multi-scale frequency components and adaptively reweights them to enhance structural details. The Fine-grained Frequency-domain Collaboration (FFC) module captures long-range spatial dependencies and promotes global contextual interaction. The Style-Adaptive Refinement (SAR) module performs style-aware feature correction based on global statistics, reducing inter-modal discrepancies and improving spectral-spatial consistency. Extensive evaluations across both simulated and real-world datasets confirm the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed EFSANet over state-of-the-art methods in preserving fine spatial details and spectral consistency.

title: A Fully-Integrated CMOS Gate Driver for a GaN-Based Half-Bridge in 48V/12V Bidirectional Automotive Power Conversion
authors: Xinyi Li, Siqin Hua, Yuanchi Cai, Yuan Gao
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Bidirectional DC/DC converters are essential for efficient power distribution in electric vehicles. Gallium nitride (GaN) devices enable high power density as switching elements, but their intrinsic characteristics impose stringent requirements on the reliability of GaN drivers. This paper proposes a fully-integrated half-bridge GaN driver, which enables bidirectional power transfer between 12V and 48V systems. A three-level/bipolar GaN gate driver enhances system reliability under high dv/dt conditions. The integrated charge-sharing bootstrap circuit achieves a compact design and reliable operation of the high-side device. The adaptive dead-time modulation provides an adjustable delay under different load conditions to minimize the dead-time loss. The proposed GaN driver is fabricated in a 0.18μm BCD process with a die area of 3.654mm2. Design considerations in the layout and PCB implementation are discussed. Measurement results demonstrate that at a 5MHz operating frequency, the driver achieves bidirectional conversion between 12V and 48V. The peak efficiencies are 94.6% for the 18V-to-12V conversion and 94.18% for the 12V-to-18V conversion. Experimental results show that the design can achieve a maximum output power of 22.12W in buck mode and 22.06W in boost mode.

title: PriRAG: Privacy-Preserving Inference for Retrieval-Augmented Large Language Models
authors: Huili Wang, Yuanhong Huang, Wenjie Zheng, Pingyi Fan, Guoshun Nan, Shangguang Wang, Mengwei Xu, Tao Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) services enhances LLMs by dynamically retrieving relevant information from external knowledge sources, addressing their limitations in handling up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge. Under the constraints of the cloud-native LLM service paradigm, many existing RAG systems are typically deployed in cloud environments and rely on retrieving raw information from local datasets of users or third-party knowledge providers to enhance generation. However, these cloud-based RAG services often neglect critical privacy and intellectual property concerns of the knowledge base, potentially leaking sensitive information during deployment or inference. To address these risks, we propose a privacy preserving framework (PriRAG) via proxy knowledge for cloud based RAG. The core idea is to compress the raw data of the retrieved information into proxy knowledge representations with carefully-designed privacy preservation mechanisms. We first propose a knowledge construction mechanism that aligns proxy representations with the semantic space of LLMs to preserve utility. Then we develop a query-centric knowledge representation method with a tailored training strategy to selectively exclude the irrelevant content, further reducing the exposure of privacy sensitive content. We further formulate a differential privacy enhanced collaborative inference strategy to secure the whole framework with certifiable guarantees. Extensive experiments on 7 public benchmarks and 4 LLMs show that PriRAG achieves a favorable balance between utility and privacy, significantly reducing privacy risks while maintaining high-quality generation.

title: AL-KalmanNet: Neural Network-Aided Kalman Filtering Based on Active Learning
authors: Andi Lin, Jianming Ruan, Wen-An Zhang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Neural network-aided Kalman filters (KFs) can learn from data to reliably estimate states in dynamic systems with complex and partially known dynamics. A key characteristic of current neural network-aided KF designs is their reliance on large amounts of labeled data. However, acquiring suitable datasets with a sufficient number of labeled data points consumes significant manpower, making it impractical for real-world applications. Thus, we propose Active Learning-KalmanNet (AL-KalmanNet), a neural network-aided KF that integrates recent advances in deep learning-based filtering into the active learning framework in a practical manner. In AL-KalmanNet, an uncertainty-based acquisition function is developed to select the most informative unlabeled data points for querying, enabling neural network-aided KFs to learn from small amounts of labeled data and achieve performance comparable to that of neural network-aided KFs trained with sufficient data. Numerical simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Obstacle Avoidance of a Snake Robot by Partial Deformation of Crawler Gait for Overhead and Lateral Constraints
authors: Hana Kumakura, Ching Wen Chin, Motoyasu Tanaka
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Wheel-less snake robots can traverse rough terrain using crawler gait. In this study, the target curve for the crawler gait is partially deformed so that the robot can traverse confined spaces without contacting overhead or lateral obstacles. Four deformation methods with different slippage characteristics are proposed. The effectiveness of the proposed methods was evaluated through real-robot experiments in planar and random-step environments, focusing on slippage, the pass-through success rate, and locomotion duration. The experimental results confirmed that the proposed methods enable effective confined-space pass-through motion on rough terrain. In particular, the deformation methods designed with slippage suppression in mind not only reduced slippage but also increased the pass-through success rate and shortened the locomotion duration.

title: Residual Rotation Correction Using Tactile Equivariance
authors: Yizhe Zhu, Zhang Ye, Boce Hu, Haibo Zhao, Yu Qi, Dian Wang, Robert Platt
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Visuotactile policy learning augments vision-only policies with tactile input, facilitating touch-reliant manipulation tasks where tactile sensing is essential. However, collecting demonstrations that cover diverse object orientations within the gripper can be expensive, makes sample efficiency the key requirement for developing visuotactile policies. We present EquiTac, a framework that leverages the planar SO(2) rotational symmetry of an object held by a parallel gripper to improve sample efficiency and generalization for visuotactile policy learning. EquiTac reconstructs surface normal maps from RGB tactile images to obtain a rotation-consistent representation. An <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rm{SO}(2)$</tex-math></inline-formula>-equivariant network predicts a yaw residual from a single tactile frame, which is composed with the output of a pretrained visuomotor policy to correct orientation errors at test time. On a real robot, EquiTac accurately achieves robust generalization to unseen object orientations within grippers using very few training samples, where baselines fail even with more training data. By explicitly encoding <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rm{SO}(2)$</tex-math></inline-formula> tactile equivariance, EquiTac provides a lightweight residual correction mechanism for orientation-sensitive parallel-gripper manipulation.

title:
BEOL-Compatible NV-SRAM With Stacked ITO FETs and HfO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    -Based Ferroelectric Capacitors
authors: Jiawei Xie, Zuopu Zhou, Leming Jiao, Yuye Kang, Kaizhen Han, Zijie Zheng, Qiwen Kong, Xiaolin Wang, Bich-Yen Nguyen, Xiao Gong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: In this work, we experimentally demonstrated a back-end-of-line (BEOL)-compatible nonvolatile SRAM (NV-SRAM) leveraging HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-based metal/ferroelectric/metal (MFM) capacitors for nonvolatility and ITO-channel FETs for enhanced integration density. With a maximum process temperature of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$400~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C, our NV-SRAM can be seamlessly integrated into BEOL, enabling monolithic 3-D vertical stacking of multilayer memories above silicon CMOS circuits for ultrahigh density and bandwidth. We successfully demonstrated store and recall operations in the fabricated NV-SRAM cell, which eliminates standby power consumption using FE capacitors and operates at a low VDD of 1.5V with a plate line (PL) voltage of 2 V. Experimental evaluations of the cell with indium-tin-oxide (ITO) FETs featuring a 75-nm channel length, along with SPICE simulations of ultrascaled cells, underscore the significant potential of BEOL NV-SRAM for data-centric computing.

title: Segmented Linear Regression Modeling for Interpretable AC Core Loss Prediction in Magnetic Powder Cores
authors: Sho Muroga, Shunsuke Matsumoto, Yudai Kodama, Saijian Ajia, Yasushi Endo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: A simple and interpretable machine learning approach was proposed to predict AC core loss in magnetic powder cores fabricated from electrolytic iron powders, aiming at the optimization of process conditions for material design. Nineteen types of toroidal cores were fabricated under various process parameters, and their AC core losses were measured. A dataset consisting of these process parameters, measurement conditions, and measured AC core losses was prepared. Based on empirical rules regarding physical loss mechanisms, the dataset was segmented and logarithmically transformed. Then, two Lasso regression models were trained using the respective datasets. The trained models achieved a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.998), demonstrating predictive performance comparable to complex non-linear black-box algorithms. Furthermore, the trained regression models successfully quantified the relationship between the AC core loss and individual process parameters. These results demonstrate the potential of the proposed machine learning methodology to accelerate the material design to achieve target performances.

title: Distance Measuring under Water with Increased Accuracy and Improved Design
authors: Elena I. Shirokova, Nikita A. Kosarev, Igor B. Shirokov
publish: 2025 IEEE Ocean Engineering Technology and Innovation Conference: Management and Conservation for Sustainable and Resilient Marine and Coastal Resources (OETIC)
abstract: The technology for measuring the distance under water with increased accuracy is considered. It is proposed to simultaneously emit of two hydro acoustic signals with different frequencies. At the same time, it is proposed to emit two modulated electromagnetic signals with different high frequencies. Modulation signals have the same frequencies as hydro acoustic ones. By measuring of phase differences of received and demodulated high frequency signals and received hydro acoustic signals it is possible to determine the distance under water with high accuracy.

authors: Salman Khalil, Iván Tlacaélel Franco-González, Eric Fung, Yao Zhang, Bin Zheng, Roman Bednarik, Hana Vrzakova

title: Automated geometric compliance assessment of barrier-free ramps based on LiDAR data_supp1-3697078.docx
authors: Qian Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Barrier-free ramps in public facilities are still predominantly inspected through manual measurement, making compliance checking labor-intensive, inconsistent, and difficult to scale, especially for existing facilities with complex layouts or without BIM models. To address this practical gap, this study proposes a LiDAR-based automated framework for the geometric compliance assessment of barrier-free ramps using only point cloud data. The framework standardizes multi-station scans, semantically identifies platform surfaces, ramp surfaces, and handrails, extracts regulation-oriented geometric parameters, and directly converts them into clause-level compliance results and standardized inspection reports. In this way, the proposed method is intended not only for geometric measurement, but also for routine compliance auditing of existing facilities by non-specialist personnel (e.g. facility managers) without requiring specialist interpretation of barrier-free codes. Tests on twelve outdoor barrier-free ramps with diverse geometric layouts show robust performance under scanning noise and reliable handling of multi-segment ramps and different handrail configurations. Compared with manual inspection, the framework provides more standardized and repeatable compliance outputs and achieves a maximum inspection efficiency improvement of 27.32% for geometrically complex ramps. These results demonstrate the practical usefulness of the proposed method for large-scale barrier-free facility auditing, retrofit prioritization, and digital infrastructure management.

title: Verifiable and Privacy-Preserving Multidimensional Range-Aggregate Queries for Spatio-Temporal Data
authors: Bingyue Shen, Kai Zhang, Jianting Ning, Lifei Wei
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The large-scale deployment of Internet of Things (IoT) devices continuously generates spatio-temporal sensing data associated with time and location, which promotes the demand for privacy-preserving range-aggregate queries over such data. However, existing range-aggregate queries for IoT data only support aggregate computations over a single dimension, and fail to perform verification over range query result and aggregate computation. In this paper, we present VeriMIRA, a privacy-preserving range-aggregate query system over spatio-temporal IoT data with multidimensional numerical attributes. Technically, for range queries over outsourced spatio-temporal IoT data, we introduce new primitives of spatio-temporal prefix encoding (STPE) and verifiable and searchable spatio-temporal EF-Tree (VSEF-Tree), and combine improved symmetric homomorphic encryption (iSHE).For aggregate computation over multidimensional attributes, we introduce a new primitive of multi-client functional encryption with verifiable aggregation for inner product (VA-MCFE). By employing VSEF-Tree and VA-MCFE, the results of both range query and aggregation computation can be efficiently verified. In addition, we present security analysis on the introduced new primitives and VeriMIRA. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments, and evaluate the performance of existing solutions and VeriMIRA over a real-world dataset, which demonstrates practical computation and communication overhead for spatio-temporal range-aggregate queries. With sacrificing only 20% overhead in the data outsourcing stage, VeriMIRA achieves 70% time savings over state-of-the-art range-aggregate query approaches at 20,000 aggregations, while providing range-aggregate verification property.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Deep Learning for Antimicrobial Resistance Prediction: A Scoping Review of Architectures and Explainability
authors: Yasmin Shehata, Dhouha Ben Noureddine
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a critical global challenge, driven by the widespread misuse of antibiotics across human, animal, and environmental domains. Conventional laboratory-based antimicrobial susceptibility testing is time-consuming and resource-intensive, limiting its scalability and clinical responsiveness. Artificial intelligence (AI) and deep learning (DL) have emerged as effective computational tools for predicting AMR from heterogeneous data sources. This scoping review systematically maps AI- and DL-based methodologies for AMR prediction across 20 peer-reviewed studies published between 2021 and 2025. Following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, we analyze methodological trends, strengths, and limitations across three thematic clusters: genomic and spectrometric prediction, clinical and EHR-based prediction, and proteomic antibiotic resistance gene detection. A three-generation architectural progression is observed, from classical machine learning and CNN-based models, through XAI-integrated CNNs functioning as biological discovery tools, to large language model and foundation model approaches enabling few-shot adaptation to emerging antibiotics. Seven distinct explainability methods are identified, of which four achieve formally validated attributions and five demonstrate biologically grounded explanations, with partial overlap between categories. Key challenges include limited data standardization, absence of temporal and geographic external validation, heterogeneous and unvalidated explainability practices, and real-world deployment constraints. This review provides a structured technical synthesis of AI-driven AMR prediction methodologies and outlines future research priorities toward multi-species evaluation, temporally validated models, standardized explainability reporting, and clinically deployable systems aligned with the One Health paradigm.

title: An Optimized FLOD-FDTD Method for Transverse-Magnetic Waves in Lossy Media
authors: Yaxiu Sun, Guangyu Zhang, Mengjie Zhang, Junyan Wang, Dawei Zhang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter proposes an optimized fundamental locally 1-D finite-difference time-domain (FLOD-FDTD) method for simulating transverse-magnetic (TM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">z</sub>) waves in lossy media. To address the limitation where numerical accuracy inherently depends on the time-step resolution of the material relaxation constant—an issue persisting in TM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">z</sub> waves even with time-weighted factors—this letter introduces scaling factors for the permittivity, permeability, and conductivity to reduce both the phase and attenuation errors. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly reduce phase and attenuation errors within a wide conductivity range from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-6}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{6}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> S/m. In this way, the optimized FLOD-FDTD method not only inherits the high computational efficiency of the FLOD-FDTD method but also possesses high-precision characteristics in lossy media.

title: High-Precision Electromagnetic Acoustic Inspection Based on Arc-Focused Phased Array and Phase-Coherent Synthetic Aperture Imaging
authors: Shen Shan, Yang Fang, Yuange Zhang, Jie Deng, Yinqiang Qu, Hong-En Chen, Cuixiang Pei, Zhenmao Chen
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: To address the challenges of low excitation efficiency, weak echo signals and limited detection accuracy in electromagnetic acoustic transducers (EMATs) for defect detection in metal plates, this paper proposes a high-precision detection method integrating arc-shaped focused phased arrays with phase-coherent synthetic aperture imaging compared to traditional methods. First, an arc-shaped phased array EMAT is constructed. Through array geometric focusing and superposition of array elements, ultrasonic energy undergoes dual enhancement, thereby increasing the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of defect echo signals. Second, based on phase-coherent synthetic aperture imaging principles, propagation delay compensation and phase correction are applied to multi-channel echo signals. This enables coherent accumulation and virtual focusing of echo energy, effectively improving imaging accuracy and resolution. Furthermore, compressed sensing theory is integrated into the imaging framework. By establishing a sparse representation model for defect signals, acceptable image recovery is achieved even with a limited number of receiving channels. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed arc-shaped focusing phased array combined with phase-coherent synthetic aperture imaging improves detection accuracy and image quality. The compressed sensing extension strategy reduces channel requirements while preserving acceptable imaging performance under half-channel sparse sampling conditions. This research provides a novel technical approach for high-precision and high-efficiency EMAT non-destructive testing.

title: RCodSpace: A Robust Learned Coding Method for Deep Space Visual Transmission_supp1-3695654.pdf
authors: Chen Cui
publish: N/A
abstract: With advancement of deep space exploration, vast amounts of image data must be transmitted back to Earth for scientific research. Current deep-space image codecs rely on conventional progressive coding algorithms, but offer limited compression performance. Despite great success on natural images achieved by learning-based compression methods, the high computational complexity restrains their application in the deep space missions and they are unable to cope with packet loss caused by severe noise interference during transmission. Motivated by the urgent need and technical challenges, we take Mars as a representative case and propose a novel image compression and transmission framework that innovatively incorporates learning-based strategies to deliver both low-complexity and error-resilient source coding. To adapt learning-based methods to the stringent constraints and high packet loss of deep space environment, we first establish a new Martian image dataset with high resolution and diversity, and analyze its characteristics to guide the network design. With heterogeneous textures yet synergistic structures as well as higher inter-channel similarity in the feature domain revealed for the Martian images, we develop a Martian Vision Adaptive Transformation Module (MVATM) with efficient low-complexity compression. Furthermore, unlike conventional one-stage training, a novel two-stage training strategy with Joint Channel Training (JCT) is proposed to enhance error resilience. Experimental results and hardware deployment strongly validate that our method achieves a better rate–distortion–complexity (RDC) trade-offs than other advanced learning-based models and significantly outperforms conventional methods in deep space simulation test. Also, the technical strategies proposed herein can offer methodological insights for deep space and other resource-constrained fields.

title: From Quantum Promise to Quantum Practice
authors: Yuan Liu, Gregory T. Byrd
publish: Computer
abstract: A cross-sector panel on quantum computing suggests that the field’s next milestone will not be another headline about qubit counts but the quieter and more consequential moment when quantum methods begin to fit credibly into real computational workflows.

title: Energy-Efficient UAV–IRS Coordination in Vehicular Networks Using GNN-Enhanced MADRL
authors: Laleh Eslami, Behrouz Shahgholi Ghahfarokhi, Mohammad Reza Khayyambashi, Pin-Han Ho
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: This paper investigates the joint optimization of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) deployment, intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) configuration, and user association in vehicular networks, aiming to maximize downlink energy efficiency under dynamic large-scale topologies. The problem is formulated as a mixed-integer non-convex program that captures coupled UAV positioning, zero-forcing (ZF) beamforming, IRS phase-shift tuning, and user scheduling, subject to mobility and power constraints. To tackle its complexity, we propose a hybrid iterative framework: UAV beamforming is optimized via ZF precoding, while the remaining variables are updated using a graph neural network (GNN)-enhanced multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) scheme under centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE). The GNN captures spatial–relational dependencies among UAVs, IRS panels, and vehicles, enabling scalable policy learning in high-mobility environments. Simulation results on realistic vehicular mobility traces reveal that the proposed method achieves notable gains in energy efficiency and convergence speed compared to conventional MARL and optimization-based baselines.

title: Sun-Outage-Aware Topology Modeling and Adaptive Routing for Optical Satellite Networks
authors: Kunpeng Zheng, Huibin Zhang, Yongli Zhao, Yuan Cao, Wei Wang, Xin Li, Zhuangzhuang Ma, Lihan Zhao, Jie Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Optical satellite networks, supported by optical inter-satellite links (OISLs), provide reliable and low-latency optical connectivity. However, periodic and predictable sun outage events significantly compromise OISL availability, leading to frequent OISL interruptions and reduced network reliability. Existing routing algorithms often overlook the regularity of sun outage-induced interrupts and their differentiated impacts on services, resulting in degraded service performance. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a sun outage-enhanced time discretization OISL model and introduces a sun outage link-aware routing (SOLR) algorithm. By incorporating joint awareness of sun outage patterns and service requirements, SOLR employs an adaptive optimization mechanism to dynamically adjust routing decisions within temporal windows. Experimental results demonstrate that SOLR extends stable path durations by 39.9%, reduces interruption rates by 28.5%, and decreases blocking rates by 36.4%, significantly outperforming link-state-based routing algorithms. By effectively mitigating the impact of sun outages, SOLR ensures continuous optical service connections. This interruption-tolerant framework bridges network modeling and service provisioning, offering a robust solution for mission-critical service in optical satellite networks.

title: A Three-Degree-of-Freedom Cooperative Optimization Modulation Strategy for Reducing Inductor RMS Current in TS-DAB Converter
authors: Jingyan Wang, Junchen Yan, Xuan Zhang, Changsong Chen, Shanxu Duan
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Three-phase single-stage DAB-type ac-dc converters (TS-DAB converters) offer high efficiency, power density, and reliability. Consequently, they present an attractive alternative to three-phase ac-dc converters in applications requiring isolation and bidirectional power flow. In order to solve the problem of excessive RMS current in the inductor of TS-DAB converter, this paper proposes a three degree of freedom optimized modulation strategy. This method is based on a five segment SVM+PSM modulation strategy, establishing a Fourier series mathematical model of inductor current, decoupling the modulation index m, using the primary side voltage duty cycles d1 and d2 and phase shift angle ϕ as control degrees of freedom, and using offline optimization and real-time lookup table methods to determine the optimal combination of minimizing inductor RMS current. Build a 1kW experimental platform for verification. The RMS current of the proposed method's inductance was compared with the traditional fixed modulation index m method. The experimental results confirm that this method can effectively reduce the RMS current of inductance.

title: Hierarchical Game-Theoretic Control for Distributed Power Quality Systems Under Capacity Constraints
authors: Yudong Du, Jingjing Huang, Aimin Zhang, Shan Wang, Xuming Gao, Yifan Zhang, Lei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: To mitigate the massive and spatially dispersed power quality (PQ) disturbances induced by renewable energy generation and nonlinear loads directly at their source, overcome the inherent capability deficiencies of conventional centralized and on-site mitigation modes, and reduce the additional losses from long-distance disturbance transmission, this article proposes a terminal-side distributed power quality control system (DPQCS) driven by a novel hierarchical game-theoretic control strategy. By deploying numerous compact and modular intelligent power quality control units (IPQCUs) at terminal distribution cabinets closest to the sources, this architecture establishes a dedicated distributed cyber–physical system (CPS) for PQ mitigation, successfully shifting the mitigation boundary to the disturbance sources. Furthermore, to address the operational scenarios where terminal mitigation nodes face insufficient compensation capacity induced by sustained load fluctuations, the proposed hierarchical game-theoretic strategy plays a pivotal role. The primary tier guarantees strict PQ standard compliance via a minimum-capacity sufficiency model, while the secondary tier employs a cooperative loss-weighted game model to dynamically optimize residual resource allocation, thereby minimizing transmission losses. Simulation results demonstrate the architectural superiority of the DPQCS over existing methods. Additionally, experimental results confirm that the proposed control strategy prioritizes full PQ compliance and reduces transmission losses by 17.6% and 33.9% compared to conventional direct control and priority-based methods, respectively, providing a highly efficient solution for modern industrial microgrids.

title: [Invited] Integrated Sensing and Communication over Optical Fiber: A Communication-centric Cross-layer View from Long-haul to Short-reach Networks
authors: Jingchuan Wang, Maoqi Liu, Liwang Lu, Huan He, Changyuan Yu, Alan Pak Tao Lau, Chao Lu
publish: IEEE Photonics Journal
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication over optical fiber (ISACoF) has emerged as a promising approach to transform widely deployed fiber infrastructures from passive transmission media into self-aware multifunctional platforms. However, the design requirements of ISACoF vary substantially across network layers, from long-haul and metro/data-center-interconnect (DCI) links to short-reach access and radio access networks (RANs), and a unified system-level perspective is still lacking. In this paper, we present a cross-layer view of ISACoF and review a series of our experimentally validated architectures developed for different transmission scenarios. For long-haul and metro/DCI networks, we address key challenges including sensing-induced nonlinear crosstalk, the integration of forward and backscattering sensing mechanisms, and endogenous sensing enabled by communication channels. Specifically, we investigate the coexistence of DAS and coherent communication in dense wavelength-division multiplexing (DWDM) systems, forward-polarization-sensing-triggered area-focused DAS, and optical-supervisory-channel-based sensing utilizing live data streams. For short-reach access networks and RANs, we target unresolved issues such as sensing trace aliasing in fiber access networks, leveraging legacy infrastructure for sensing, and enabling sensing functionality in fiber-wireless converged systems. To this end, we explore dual-comb-enabled multipath sensing in passive optical networks (PONs), Fresnel-reflection-assisted branch monitoring, seamless sensing in radio-over-fiber (RoF) links, orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) fronthaul with embedded linear frequency-modulated pilots for large dynamic-range vibration detection, and fiber-wireless collaborative sensing. By organizing these studies under a communication-centric framework, we extract the key tradeoffs among transmission reach, spatial resolution, sensing sensitivity, implementation complexity, and communication penalty, and explore the benefits of ISACoF toward intelligent, low-cost, and multifunctional optical networks.

title: Decentralized Adaptive Initial Congestion Window in 5G Using Handshake-Based Flow Classification and Online Learning
authors: S Gangadhar, A Chinmayananda, Animesh Roy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The growing prevalence of latency-sensitive applications necessitates increasingly adaptive network congestion control mechanisms. This is particularly critical in Next-Generation Networks, e.g., 5G and beyond 5G networks, which promise low-latency and high-throughput support for diverse traffic. Traditional TCP variants employ a fixed Initial Congestion Window (ICW), resulting in suboptimal performance for short and long flows. This is because a small, fixed ICW unnecessarily prolongs short flows, while also delaying the ramp-up of long flows, leading to under-utilized bandwidth and increased latency. While machine learning based solutions offer improvements, they often rely on centralized architectures, limiting their applicability in decentralized scenarios. This paper introduces a novel decentralized framework that combines lightweight machine learning -based flow classification with online learning for real-time adaptive ICW. We first generate a unique dataset using OMNeT++/Simu5G simulations and select a suitable classifier that leverages TCP handshake features to distinguish short flows from long flows with 97.1% accuracy. This prediction drives an online learning model to dynamically select the efficient ICW before data transmission. Rigorous evaluation across TCP variants such as Reno, NewReno, and Westwood through simulations shows that our proposed framework achieves a 27–36% reduction in flow completion time and a 104-204% increase in throughput compared to baseline implementations. Robustness of our framework is further validated via an ablation study, threshold sensitivity analysis, missing TCP options, statistical significance with 95% confidence intervals (10-seed experiments, p < 0.0001), and multi-UE fairness (Jain index > 0.99). Operating entirely at the UE, our proposed solution eliminates dependence on centralized control, offering a scalable and resilient strategy for Next-Generation Networks.

title: A Modality-Mixing Fusion Network for Accurate Land Use/Land Cover Segmentation
authors: Ayesha Irfan, Yu Li, Guangmin Sun, Andrea Buono, Maurizio Migliaccio, Dequn Zhao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate land use and land cover (LULC) segmentation from remote sensing images is consistently challenging, especially when integrating different data sources such as optical and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images. Although earlier multimodal approaches have shown promising results, many do not sufficiently utilize fine-grained cross-modal relationships or adaptively manage the spatial and semantic variability found in complex and diverse LULC categories. To address these challenges, we introduce MMixF-Net (Modality-Mixing Fusion Network), a novel deep convolutional architecture specifically designed for multimodal LULC segmentation. Our proposed model features a multistage fusion process with a modality-mixing tokenizer for early patch-level alignment, a cross-modality attention mechanism, and a gating module for dynamic feature interaction. An adaptive decoder with attention-guided skip connections is also incorporated to improve boundary accuracy and class distinction. Evaluations on the WHU-OPT-SAR dataset indicate that our model achieves an overall accuracy (OA) of 89.4%, a mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) of 52.8%, and a Kappa coefficient of 75.7%, surpassing several recent benchmarks. These results highlight the effectiveness of MMixF-Net to effectively integrate multisource remote sensing data and highlight its significance in advancing machine learning-based geospatial analysis and environmental mapping.

title: Identifying Critical Breakpoints and Latency Effects During Remote Backup Driving
authors: Xingjian Ma, Anthony D. McDonald
publish: IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems
abstract: As Level 4 driving automation progresses, remote driving remains a critical fallback for commercial trucks operating at the edge of their operational design domains. However, network latency presents a major challenge, impairing driving performance, increasing cognitive workload, and reducing trust in the remote driving system. This study investigates how latency affects human factors and driving performance in remote driving scenarios through a within-subject driving simulator experiment. Results showed that latency significantly increased perceived workload and reduced trust, with no direct effect on fatigue or situation awareness. Driving performance was also affected: median speed decreased, steering corrections intensified, and steering entropy increased under higher latency. Segment analysis revealed that lane-keeping tasks—particularly in curved road segments—were most sensitive to latency, exhibiting the strongest degradation in control stability. In contrast, lane-changing maneuvers were less affected, likely due to their discrete and anticipatory nature. Segmented regression identified critical latency breakpoints (347–480 ms), beyond which trust in the remote driving system and driving performance exhibited substantial changes. These findings highlight the need for latency-aware, context-sensitive design strategies, including predictive assistance, and adaptive feedback interfaces, to support safe and efficient remote truck operation under fluctuating network conditions.

title: Connection-Constrained Genetic Algorithms for Feasible Multi-Objective Vehicle Powertrain Architecture Optimization
authors: Stefanos G. Zafiris, Rohail A. Malik, Muhammad Ahmad, Jari Vepsäläinen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents two feasibility-by-construction evolutionary algorithms for automated vehicle powertrain architecture optimization, the Connection-Constrained Genetic Algorithm (CC-GA) and its multi-objective extension NSGA-CC. Both methods embed component-level connection types and directions directly into the genetic encoding and apply constraint-aware crossover together with a Large Neighborhood Search based regenerative destroy-and-repair mutation. This formulation ensures that only architectures satisfying strict physical and directional compatibility rules are generated and that all candidates are directly simulatable within a MATLAB Simulink framework capable of assembling variable-length powertrains. The proposed algorithms are benchmarked against a random feasible generator, a modified genetic algorithm, a modified NSGA-II sharing the same constraint-aware crossover but using parameter level mutation, and standalone large neighborhood search. Experiments span multiple error acceptance thresholds, stochastic seeds, and design space sizes, including MAE ≤ 1 and MAE ≤ 10 and parameter variation levels of 0.15, 0.30, and 0.45. Performance is evaluated by the number of simulations required to obtain 50 accepted designs, together with hypervolume and IGD+ computed on globally normalized objectives including MAE, cost, energy-specific consumption, and emissions. Results show that CC-GA produces wide and well converged solution fronts under strict performance limits, while NSGA-CC provides the strongest multi-objective trade off quality as constraints are relaxed and the design space expands. Although the modified GA and NSGA-II remain the most evaluation efficient methods in terms of raw simulation count, the connection-constrained approaches achieve the best overall balance between solution quality and computational efficiency, demonstrating their suitability for large, discrete, and highly constrained engineering design problems, especially catalog-based architecture synthesis problems.

title: A Low-Profile Wideband Patch Antenna with Multi-Mode Tuning for 5G Bands(N77/N78/N79)
authors: Wenjie Wang, Yu Shao
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Limited frame space on mobile devices motivates researchers to investigate the back-cover area to relieve pressure on the terminal frame. This work focuses on the ample space available on the back-cover and proposes a compact wideband patch antenna featuring a dual-layer shorting-via structure and a cross-shaped feeding network for 5G-NR N77/N78/N79 applications. Specifically, the roles of the shorting vias and the feeding network are analyzed separately: the outer vias introduce additional inductive loading and enable TM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub>-like mode, while the inner vias primarily tune the probe-excited monopole mode with negligible impact on the inherent TM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sub> mode. An even-mode-driven cross-feeding network excites an auxiliary TM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">10</sub>-like mode and provides an effective parallel resonant path to improve impedance matching. The proposed patch antenna achieves four resonances within the passband. Fabrication and measurement show a -6 dB impedance bandwidth from 3.25 to 5.0 GHz (42%) with an ultra-thin profile of 2.0mm, corresponding to 0.022 λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">g</sub> (where λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">g</sub> is the guided wavelength at 3.3 GHz). The prototype demonstrates a peak gain of 7.9 dBi and a total efficiency of 41%–81%, with measured radiation patterns in good agreement with simulations. The proposed antenna achieves a favorable tradeoff among wide bandwidth, low profile, and high gain without relying on external bandwidth-enhancement structures. This makes it a competitive and practical solution for integration into smartphones and other compact 5G-NR terminal devices.

title: Two-Phase Optimization via Dynamic Marginal Gain: An Interval Pre-Scheduling Approach for Space-to-Space Surveillance
authors: Di Yang, Shuai Guo, Ming Ming Li, Jia Cheng Zhang, Ya Zhong Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Low Earth orbit constellation networking surveillance has great potential for space target surveillance (STS). Efficiently scheduling onboard sensors is crucial for enhancing mission performance. Addressing the challenges in pre-scheduling for STS— namely, a discontinuous feasible solution space, complex intertwined constraints, and the necessity of balancing multiple performance metrics— this paper constructs a preemptive interval pre-scheduling (PIPS) model. To solve it, a dynamic marginal gain-based two-phase optimization (DMG-TPO) method is proposed. First, a progressive optimization framework based on constraint enhancement-relaxation is developed for strongly coupled combinatorial interval scheduling problems. This framework decouples heterogeneous decision spaces and balances solution quality with computational efficiency by rapidly constructing feasible solutions and precisely refining interval boundaries. Then, a hybrid encoding scheme is designed to achieve implicit constraint avoidance through direct mapping from decision variables to the feasible solution space. In particular, this scheme decouples the decision space from the mission scale, ensuring the algorithmic scalability. Subsequently, a dynamic marginal gain estimation model is proposed. Considering the hierarchical relationship among multiple metrics, this model performs as a local decoder and guides the local decision by evaluating analytical increments of global indicators, thereby overcoming the inherent myopia of static heuristics. Through comprehensive numerical simulations, the effectiveness of STS in different scenarios was evaluated, and the performance and sensitivity of the algorithm were verified. Compared with conventional methods, the proposed method achieves an overall improvement in performance of approximately 27%∼38%. Runtime analysis further shows at least 6-fold faster convergence to the benchmark objective, enabling second- to minute-level responses for large-scale constellation scheduling.

title: Variable Dual Time Scale Caching Update and Resource Allocation Scheme for UAV-Assisted Edge Networks
authors: Taijun Peng, Chao Yang, Qiutian Xu, Xin Chen, Yi Liu
publish: IEEE Journal on Miniaturization for Air and Space Systems
abstract: Leveraging wireless caching in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs)-assisted edge networks, UAVs can pre-cache part of popular contents and fly to the users directly, the repeated backhaul transmission delays are reduced. However, the limited caching space, complex network environments and varying user demands make a challenge for designing an effective caching content selection and update scheme. The caching update of UAVs introduces an additional cost, and UAVs can move to the suitable locations in real time to improve the air-to-ground transmission rates. In this paper, we propose a Variable Dual time scale Caching Update and Resource Allocation scheme (VD-CURA) for UAV-assisted edge networks, to minimize the average system task processing latency per unit content size. A joint optimization problem is proposed that the caching update for UAVs, including the caching content selection and update interval are optimized at the large time scale, and the UAV trajectory optimization and bandwidth allocation are performed at each small scale. For the varying networks, a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL)-based VD-CURA framework with two layers is proposed, with combined proximal policy optimization (PPO) in the upper layer and deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) in the lower layer. Notably, we introduce a new caching content selection metric Experimental results demonstrate the robust convergence and adaptability of the proposed VD-CURA solution algorithm, compared with the baseline schemes, the average latency per unit of content is reduced significantly.
